[1] Influenza Virus - Structure and Function
Influenza A virus consists of several components including the envelope containing surface glycoprotein spikes (hemagglutinin and neuraminidase), transmembrane M2 ion channel protein embedded in lipid bilayer, and internal matrix protein (M1). Hemagglutinin (HA) mediates attachment to sialic acid residues on host cells and facilitates viral entry into host cells. Neuraminidase (NA) cleaves terminal sialic acids from newly synthesized viral particles enabling release from infected cells. The matrix protein M1 plays a critical role in organizing the viral ribonucleoprotein core during assembly and budding. Mutations in HA and NA contribute significantly to antigenic drift phenomena. [3]
[2] Antigenic Shift vs Drift - Differences Between Influenza Viruses
Antigenic drift is characterized by gradual accumulation of amino acid substitutions in hemagglutinin (HA) and neuraminidase (NA) proteins due to point mutations throughout replication cycles. Such changes alter antigenicity and allow escape from neutralizing antibodies developed against older strains. Antigenic shift involves abrupt change in HA or NA subtypes due to reassortment between different influenza A viruses infecting the same host—a rare event causing global pandemics. [4]
[3] Molecular Biology of Influenza Virus Replication
During RNA-dependent RNA synthesis, errors accumulate in viral polymerases leading to random base pair insertions/deletions (indels); however, these indels often result in frameshifting unless corrected post-transcriptionally. Most commonly reported mutational events contributing to antigenic evolution involve point mutations rather than insertion-deletion events or frameshifts. [5]
[4] Evolutionary Dynamics of Influenza Viruses
Genetic diversity in seasonal influenza arises largely through continuous point mutations affecting functional domains of surface glycoproteins such as HA and NA. Over decades, these incremental changes accumulate until sufficient divergence leads to reduced recognition by existing sera-based immunity—an outcome termed antigenic drift. [6]
[5] Vaccination Strategies Against Seasonal Flu
Seasonal flu vaccines are updated annually based on predictions derived from surveillance systems tracking emerging variants exhibiting antigenic drift in HA and/or NA proteins. The effectiveness of annual vaccines depends heavily upon matching closely with circulating strains showing recent drift patterns. [7]
[6] Clinical Relevance of Antigenic Variation in Influenza
Drift allows persistent circulation of influenza viruses within populations despite ongoing exposure and prior immunity—this contributes substantially to recurrent epidemics worldwide. Without regular updates to vaccine formulations, mismatched vaccines reduce protection levels dramatically. [8]
